For Students Using This Template

This site is built with MkDocs Material. Everything is written in Markdown — just edit the .md files in the docs/ folder! Check the README for setup instructions. if you want to test in your local machine, use this command: - pip install mkdocs-material - mkdocs serve --livereload - Open http://127.0.0.1:8000

docs.title: Tshering Choden
docs.text:
Title / Role — student

""The only way to do great work is to love what you do." - Steve Jobs"

I was born on 6th of June, 2009. I am from central part of Bhutan called Bumthang which is aslo known as the cultural heartland of Bhutan. I have a lot of hobbies and interests. I love playing football and volleyball. Moreover, I also enjoy reading books and journaling. On top of that, I love spending time with my loved ones and just chat with them.

I am someone who has a lot of hobbies. I am very interested in playing sports. I see sports as a way to connect with other people and built lasting relationships and memories. I am also very interested in learning about psychology because I am fascinated about knowing human's mind and behaviors.

Moreover, I have also took part in many competitions and represented our school.One of my favourite moments about playing sports would be being able to be a part of the ParoFC team.

One of my biggest passion would be reading fiction books as it helps me imagine different circumstances that enhances my creative thinking. My favourite book is "Harry Potter" because it is not only easy to read but also very fascinating. Moreover, reading helps me understand things that I never knew, see different places that I have never been to and experience different circumstances of lives.

docs.title: ➕ How to Add New Documents
docs.text:
Adding a New Page

  1. Create a new .md file in the docs/documentation/ folder (e.g., my-new-topic.md)

  2. Copy the content from template.md as a starting point

  3. Add your new page to mkdocs.yml under the Documentation section:

    nav:
  - Documentation:
      - Overview: documentation/index.md
      - Getting Started Guide: documentation/getting-started.md
      - "📝 Blank Template": documentation/template.md
      - My New Topic: documentation/my-new-topic.md    # ← add here

  4. Save and run mkdocs serve to see your changes!

docs.title: Learning 3D designing
docs.text:
Date: 2026-02-22

Author: Tshering Choden

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This document covers my journey of learning about 3D design and getting familiar with few softwares to do 3D design like Onshape. This document also includes few terminologies related to designing.

Photo Credit:Google

docs.title: Subtractive and Additive
docs.text:
Additive design is the process of creating a shape by building it up with layers of material. For example, while using a 3D printer you add layers until the object is finished.

Subtractive means you create a shape by removing material from a solid block, like cutting, carving, or drilling away parts to get the final design.

Photo Credit:Google

docs.title: Software used for 3D design
docs.text: Software Creation Tinkercad Cup Onshape A box and a pencil holder

docs.title: My design in Tinkercad
docs.text: Photo Credit:Tinkercad

docs.title: My design in Onshape
docs.text:
Photo Credit:Onshape

Photo Credit:Onshape

docs.title: Parametric Design
docs.text:
In parametric design, a model's size and shape are controlled by numbers, so changing a number simultaneously changes the design.

Photo Credit:Google

docs.title: What is pressfit?
docs.text:
Pressfit is a way of joining parts so they stay together without screws or glue. The parts are made to fit tightly and hold in place by friction.

Photo Credit:Google

docs.title: What is a kerf?
docs.text:
Kerf is the small amount of material that a laser removes when cutting. You need to think about it when designing parts, or they might not fit together correctly.

Photo Credit:Google

docs.title: STL, OBJ and GCODE
docs.text:
In 3D design, files like STL and OBJ store the shape of your model, and OBJ can also keep colors. G-code tells the 3D printer how to build it layer by layer. These files help turn your digital design into a real object.

Photo Credit:Oreate AI

docs.title: Visit to CERN
docs.text:
Author: Tshering Choden

Tags: CERN, Learning, Experiences

CERN stands for the European Council for Nuclear Research (derived from its original French name, Conseil Européen pour la Recherche Nucléaire).

It is the world’s largest laboratory where scientists study the tiny "building blocks" that make up everything in the universe. To do this, they use a massive underground machine called the Large Hadron Collider, which acts like a giant racetrack for particles. By zooming these particles around the track and smashing them into each other at incredibly high speeds, scientists can see what’s inside them and learn how the universe worked right after it began.

Beyond just physics, CERN is also famous for being the place where the World Wide Web was invented to help researchers share their data more easily.

docs.title: Exploration
docs.text:
Traveling to new places is far more than just a break from my school routine; for me, it represents a golden opportunity to embrace adventure and engage in unforgettable learning. In 2025, I joined a group of ten students(including myself),five from Grade 9 and five from Grade 11 along with two of our teachers, on an amazing journey to CERN in Switzerland and the historic cities of Italy. Throughout the trip, I found myself completely engaged in different activities.

Our group was very fortunate to be guided by Mr. Pranav Sharma, a former theoretical physicist. His expertise and dedicated care were invaluable; he acted as an essential mentor, introducing us to brilliant minds and helping us navigate the complexities of the region with ease.

docs.title: The Frontiers of Science
docs.text:
The visit to CERN, the European Council for Nuclear Research, was a distinctive and transformative experience. As a global hub for high-energy physics, it allowed the group to explore the mechanisms behind the universe’s greatest mysteries.

A definitive highlight was the opportunity to meet Sir John Ellis, who is a renowned British theoretical physicist.

docs.title: Exploring Art in Italy
docs.text:
The trip continued to Italy, where we saw beautiful arts. In the city of Florence(birthplace of the Renaissance,), we visited the Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore. It was so massive and beautiful that it was hard to believe someone actually built it!

We also visited the home of the famous Medici family, who were known for helping great artists and students long ago. I saw famous paintings and statues by artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo.

My favorite was the statue of David. It stands tall and looks very brave, representing the strength and independence of the city.

## Reflection

I had a wonderful exposure and I wish to get such opportunities in the future as well. I thank everyone who has made it possible for me to go to such a beautiful place. From the visit, I have learned that there is so much to explore and learn in life. I have learned new values and I have got new goals to aim for. I am going to move ahead stronger with so much behind me but lots and lots ahead of me.

docs.title: Learning about GitHub
docs.text:
Author: Tshering Choden

Tags: Learning, GitHub, Documentation

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This document contains my experience learning about GitHub, including what I’ve learned, the challenges I’ve faced, and how much I've improved. I started this journey to understand how to manage and write code, but I also wanted to learn how to build a website and create better documentation.

My documentation is designed to help one learn the basics of using repositories, including how to commit and push code the right way. I also explain about common pipeline errors and how to fix them so the project stays on track. By following my notes, I hope one can avoid simple mistakes and learn how to manage your code more easily.

Picture Credit:Google

docs.title: What is GitHub?
docs.text: GitLab is a digital workspace that stores your code, tracks every change you make so you never lose your work, and uses "robots" to automatically move your website from your computer to the live internet.It’s a one-stop shop where you can write your code, track your to-do list, and launch your site all in one place.

docs.title: My Account in GitHub
docs.text: Picture Credit:Gitlab

docs.title: Installation(Process of setting up your account)
docs.text: Sign Up: Create a free account at https://github.com/ New Project: Click "New project" and choose "Create blank project" to make a folder for your work. Add a README: Check the box to "Include a README file"—this is your project's front page. Write Content: Click "Edit" on your README and type your notes using Markdown (simple symbols for bold or lists). Save (Commit): Click "Commit changes" at the bottom to save your progress and create a history of your work. Use the Web IDE: For managing multiple pages or folders, click the "Web IDE" button to use a simple built-in code editor.

docs.title: Editing Documentation Files
docs.text:
To edit the documentation in GitLab:

I opened the project.

I clicked Web IDE.

I opened the docs folder.

I opened about/index.md.

Then I added my information.

Picture Credit:Gitlab

docs.title: Markdown
docs.text: Markdown is a simple language that uses basic symbols to format your text. In GitLab, it is the main tool used to create professional README files and project notes. By using this language, you can easily add things like bold text, lists, and headings to your documents. GitLab automatically turns those simple symbols into a clean, easy-to-read layout, making your project look professional and organized.

docs.title: Repositories(Repo)
docs.text: A repository is a specialized digital folder that acts as a "living history" of your project by saving everything every time you make a change. Unlike a normal folder, it allows you to track your progress, undo mistakes by reverting to older versions, and collaborate with others without overwriting their work. While platforms like GitLab host these repositories online, the repository itself is the core engine that keeps your files organized, safe, and synchronized across a team.

docs.title: Commit and push
docs.text: Term Meaning Commit. * A local "save" that records your changes.  Includes a short note describing what you did. Push * An "upload" that sends your saves to GitLab.  Make your work visible to your team online.

docs.title: Pipeline errors
docs.text: A pipeline error is like a "failed inspection" that happens when GitLab’s automated system finds a mistake in your code while trying to build or test it. These errors usually occur because of simple typos, missing files, or incorrect settings in your configuration. To solve them, you simply click on the red "failed" icon in GitLab to read the error message, which tells you exactly what went wrong. Once you fix the mistake on your computer, you just commit and push again to restart the inspection and get back on track.

docs.title: Mkdocs
docs.text: In GitLab, MkDocs works like an automatic website maker. When you save your notes (Markdown files) and push them to GitLab, a "pipeline" starts working in the background. This pipeline uses MkDocs to grab your files and turn them into a real, clickable website hosted on GitLab Pages. Instead of clicking through a list of messy files, one can just visit a web link to read your organized and pretty documentation.

docs.title: Challenges I faced
docs.text:
Sometimes, most of my commits failed and I was not able to make any changes to my documents.

Picture Credit:Gitlab

docs.title: Progress I made
docs.text:
After many trials and errors and learning from my friends, I was able to deploy my website and create documents.

Picture Credit:Gitlab

docs.title: Laser cutting
docs.text:
Date: 2026-02-26

Author: Tshering Choden

Tags: Laser, cutting, Documentation

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This article describes laser cutting, including how it works and the materials it can cut. It's important because laser cutting is accurate, effective, and adaptable, which makes it suitable for manufacturing special designs out of materials like wood, and plastic.

docs.title: Introduction to Laser Cutting
docs.text: Laser cutting uses a powerful light beam known as a laser to cut or form materials. It can create precise, tiny shapes that are difficult to cut by hand. Laser cutting is used to create jewelry, models, and signs in factories, workshops, and schools. It is accurate and faster than using normal cutting tools.

docs.title: How Laser Cutting Works in 5 simple explanation
docs.text:
•A powerful light beam is used by a laser cutter to carve or cut objects.

•On a computer, you make a design, and the machine follows it.

•Along the design lines, the material is burned, or melted by the laser.

•Thinner materials require less power and cut more quickly, while thicker materials require more power and cut more slowly.

•Laser cutting is very quick, accurate, and capable of creating intricate designs that are challenging to cut by hand.

Picture credit:Google

docs.title: Few materials for Laser Cutting
docs.text:
Wood (plywood, hardwood)

Acrylic / Plastic sheets

Fabric and leather

Paper, cardboard, cork

Rubber

This covers the main materials you can cut or engrave with the Fusion Edge.

Picture credit:Google

docs.title: Preparing Designs for Cutting
docs.text:
I opened Inkscape and sketched the design's shapes.

To enable the Fusion Edge to accurately follow the routes, vector lines were used.

I then sent the file to the Fusion Edge laser cutter through the PC.

Depending on the material, adjust the Fusion Edge's focus, power, and speed.

After that, we watched as the Fusion Edge precisely sliced the design after turning on the machine.

Photo credit:Google

docs.title: Safety and Maintenance
docs.text:
•Safety

Keep the area ventilated and clean.

Never leave the machine unattended.

•Maintenance

Clean the machine and lenses regularly.

Check that the laser is aligned and working.

Remove dust and leftover material after use.

docs.title: TechTrek Project
docs.text:
Tshering Choden

Tags: Learning, TechTrek, Game Creation, Documentation

docs.title: Project Title: Bhutanese Sign Language Learning Website
docs.text: Our goal is to create an interactive website that teaches people how to use Bhutanese Sign Language (BSL). Users will be able to select between two learning options, spelling and sign by typing a word into a search field on the application. While the sign option uses a video to demonstrate the entire word sign, the spelling option shows each letter in sign language. With the use of this platform, learning sign language will be easy, visible, and available to anyone.

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
1. Purpose

In daily life, communication is crucial. However, there is a communication gap between the hearing community and those who are deaf or hard of hearing in Bhutan since many people are unfamiliar with Bhutanese Sign Language. Even if the Bhutanese citizens want to learn sign language, there is a lack of teachers or proper learning platforms. This initiative aims to close this gap by developing an easy-to-use platform for learning sign language. Our goals with this website are to increase awareness, encourage inclusivity, and use technology to address a serious social issue.

  1. Learning Impact

This game helps learners improve their knowledge by allowing them to learn at their own pace, enabling practice at any time. It also fosters empathy and understanding for the deaf community and supports the development of better communication skills by teaching both finger spelling and actual Bhutanese Sign Language signs.

docs.title: Theme
docs.text:
"Technology for Inclusivity and Communication" is the project's main theme.

The project's main goals are to close communication gaps in society, facilitate inclusive learning, and advance accessibility through the use of digital tools.

docs.title: Subject Integration
docs.text:
The concept of communication and inclusiveness is captured in our game through the adoption of Bhutanese Sign Language (BSL) as the core subject to learn. The importance of learning sign language is that it ensures effective communication between individuals and people who are hearing impaired. In our game, there will be two components used for the learning process which will include finger spelling and actual signs. First, when a user inputs a word into the game, the word is broken down into individual characters or letters. Every character is then displayed in sign language in order to educate users on the sign language alphabet.

Python will be used as the main programming language to build the website.

It will perform the following functions:

-Take user input (word entered in the search box)

-Convert the word into lowercase

-Break the word into individual letters

-Match the input with stored video files

-Display the appropriate videos based on user selection

For example, if a user types “cat”:

The system will break it into letters (C, A, T)

Display individual videos for each letter in spelling mode

Show one complete video for the word “cat” in sign mode

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
-offers a user-friendly, interactive sign language learning environment

-helps people in comprehending whole signals and spelling

-promotes awareness of Sign Language in Bhutan

-promotes open conversation in society

-shows how to use Python practically to solve difficulties in the real world

docs.title: Creating a basic circuit.
docs.text:
• Lighting an LED. To lit a bulb or a LED, one important required thing is Power. Tinkercad provides six types of power supply. • Lighting LED with power supply.

Figure 1

But before that Students should learn about two types of electrical circuit. Click Here .

So in the above image, the LED id directly connected battery: if this circuit is created in TinkerCad. and if you start the simulation. The exclaimation mark represents that excessive amount of current is flowing through the LED.

Therefore, to limit the flow of current we can make use of electrical passive device called Resistor.

Figure 2

Resistor offers resistance to the flow of current through the resistor. - Resistance: Resistance is a measure of the opposition to current flow in an electrical circuit. - Resistance is measured in ohms, symbolized by the Greek letter omega (Ω).

The image below can help visulize the relation between resistance, current and voltage.

Therefore, the flow of current can be easily connected with the flow of water. Where the pressure of the waterflow can be linked with the voltage in the circuit, Resistance with the small size of cross section to limit the flow of water, which can be linked with the flow of current in a circuit.

Figure 3

Before using the resistor, we also have to understand the measuring value of a resistor. - Every Resistor has a color code that defines the level of resistance it can provide to the flow of current. To check a video click here.

Figure 4

  • Then to confirm what color code of resistor to be assign, We have to follow Ohm's Law.

docs.title: Ohm's Law
docs.text:
Ohm’s law states that the voltage across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided all physical conditions and temperature remain constant. - V = IR - I = Current - V = Voltage - R = Resistance

So For LED's, 20mA is best amount of current across the LED. - Therefore to find the value of resistance required is: - let Current (I) = 20mA - Voltage (V) = 3V

Using

V = IR

Which can be also written as

R = V/I

Therefore; $$ R = \frac{3V}{20x10^{-3} A} $$ - R = 150 Ω.

Adding R = 150 Ω in series and performint simulation

Figure 1: Aesthetic Centre

docs.title: Adding a Switch in the circuit.
docs.text:
• A switch can be used to turn on or turn off a circuit. It can used to make a circuit open (off) or closed (on).
  • In this circuit we will be using a slide switch. A slide switch has two terminal and a common terminal. The common terminal will be connected to the LED through a resistor. The other two terminal can be used to complete or break the circuit.

The image below shows the use of switch to control the glow of the LED.

Figure 1: Aesthetic Centre

  • Basically for the LED to glow the circuit should be completed (i.e the two end ot the LED terminal should be connected to the positive and negative terminal of the power)
  • When the switch is off, the LED is disconnected from power i.e both side is connected to the negative terminal of the battery.
  • When the switch is on, the circuit is completed and the LED glows.

docs.title: Schematic.
docs.text:
ThinkerCad can also be used to generate schematic from the design we make.

What is Schematic?

A schematic, or schematic diagram, is a representation of the elements of a system using abstract, graphic symbols rather than realistic pictures.

Figure

The schematic shown above is symbolic representation of the circuit we have designed earlier. Schematic can be used to learn the symbols of the components used our design. - The numbered symbols are: 1. Resistor. 2. LED 3. Switch 4. Battery.

docs.title: Introduction To Arduino.
docs.text:
Figure

What is Arduino?

Arduino is an open-source hardware and software company, project, and user community that designs and manufactures single-board microcontrollers and microcontroller kits for building digital devices.

To understand more about arduino, Click Here.

docs.title: Arduino in TinkerCad.
docs.text:
Tinkercad provides various other controllor but we would be focusing more on Arduino UNO Board. - Arduino is available is various range of board version such as Arduino UNO, Arduino Nano, Arduino Mega etc.

To start programming arduino: 1. First add the arduino Board to the design from the component list. 2. Before that lets understand the different views, we can select in the tinkercad platform.

Figure

On the top right corner, we can select the views such as:

  1. Circuit view: Where we can make design.
  2. Schematic view: Whatever circuit is designed in the circuit view can be represented with components symbols.
  3. Component List: All the components added in the circuit will be listed in this view.
  4. Code: This will let the user write codes to perform simulation. Options for Block and text programming are available.
  5. Start Simulation: Whenever a design is ready, we can test the design by clicking the start simulation button.
  6. Send To: enables users to share their work.

docs.title: Blinking LED with Arduino.
docs.text:
LED

As we have learned, Arduino has Digital and analoge pins. One of the digital pin (i.e Pin 13) has a builtin LED marked red in the picture above.

  • Therefore the key point to remember is that we will be using pin number 13 to controll the builtin LED.

The block program to blink an LED on and off with deslay of 1 second each is.

Block Code

  • First block: Will send a HIGH signal to the builtin LED pin (i.e pin number 13).
  • Second block: The first block will be executed and and it will wait for one second before it executes the next block.
  • Third block: Similar to first block, now it will send a LOW signal to the builtin LED pin.
  • Fourth Block: Wait for one second.

This block of code will repeat multiple times, until the simulation is stopped.

Another Interesting feature of Tinkercad is that it will generate a text program, which can be directly uploaded to the real arduino board.

When ever a block program is created, it will simultaneously generate a text program.

The text program for the blinking LED is given Below:

Text Code

In text programming, by default arduino code will have a void setup method and void loop method. The difference between the block code and text code is mostly the void setup() :

  • The setup method is executed on once when the a system or a code is initiated.
  • Most of defining and initializing parameter work is done in this method.
  • The parameter/pin defined here is the LED_BUILTIN (i.e pin number 13) and the function of that pin is to give output.
  • In block code, defining and initializing work is handled by the platform.

Void loop():

This method determines the kind of work and processes the arduino will consider. Once the setup method is completly executed. Then it will directly jump to the loop methode. - If you compare the line of code here and block code above, both express the same type of instructions. Just expressed in text syntax.

docs.title: Blinking external LED.
docs.text:
Till here, it has been mentioned that the builtin LED is connected to pin number 13. So we will be using the same code and will only make changes to the circuit.

  • To do that, we have learned that to power up a LED we need to make a complete circuit. That is connect anode of LED to positive terminal (here we can use pin 13 since all the instruction are executed on pin 13) of through a current limiting resistor and cathode must be connected to the negative terminal (Ground/GND in arduino).

Led Test

docs.title: Working with Servo Motor.
docs.text:
What is a servo motor? - Servo motor is a rotary actuator, which means that we can adjust the output shaft to a specific angle. And we can provide the angle through program code of other input devices.

Servo Label

  1. Case
  2. Mounting taps
  3. Output shaft
  4. Servo Horns
  5. Cable
  6. Connector

Servo Motor comes in with 3 pins for power and signal.

Figure 1: Aesthetic Centre

  1. Power (RED): Connects to 5V.
  2. Ground (Brown): Connects to ground.
  3. Signl (Orange): Connects to any of the arduino pin, which can generate pulses.

To know more about servo motors, click here.

docs.title: Connecting Servo circuit.
docs.text:
From the components list, add: - Micro Servo and - Arduino Uno R3 : our microntrollor.

Servo's can only be connected with those pins which are capable to generation pulse with modulation. Those pins can be identified from the arduion board.

PWM pins

Take this in mind disign your own circuit understanding the basic connection rules learned during previous circuits. Start by adding the components on your design space.

Components

Click on the terminal of servo and connect it to the Arduino as shown in the picture below.

Servo Circuit

To change the color of the connecting wires, on the top left cornor those options are available.

docs.title: Servo program.
docs.text:
Servo Code

From the block collection, similar to the code from blinking LED, replace the LED HIGH block with SERVO ROTATE block and insert the pin number and angles.

Finally click on start simulation to view the servo simulation.

docs.title: Where Creativity Meets Accountability
docs.text:
This guide maps out how we work, how we lead, and how we keep our space amazing. Think of the FabLab not just as a room with tools, but as a living Ecosystem where every part depends on the other.

Figure 1: Aesthetic Centre

docs.title: 🌟 1. The Core Philosophy: Learn, Lead, Leave it Better
docs.text:
Our goal is to move from being just users to innovators. We aim for a 7/10 to 10/10 accessibility rating by making the lab the most welcoming place on campus.

Proactive Leadership: Don't wait to be told. If you know how to use the Laser Cutter, teach a friend!

Figure 1: Student Led Session

The Spiral Model: Your first project won't be perfect. Start small, document it, and iterate (improve it) over time.

Figure 1: Spirial Development

docs.title: 👥 2. Role Specifications (Who Does What?)
docs.text:
To keep the gears turning, we have specific roles. No one works alone; we work as a team.

Role

Responsibility

docs.title: Team Lead :
docs.text: To lead the core team in making decision making.

docs.title: 2nd Lead :
docs.text: To support the Team Lead in decision making

docs.title: Inventory Lead
docs.text:
Take note of the items borrowed by the students and make sure all the things are place in their place

The website Inventory must be aligned to physical storage. Make sure the things are arranged well.

docs.title: Documentation Reviewer :
docs.text: Fab Academy Graduates or vertifed users for a specific process.

docs.title: 💻 3. Digital Management: Aesthetic Centre Website
docs.text: Our website should ease the workflow of the FabLab. We don't issue or receive things until the digital steps are done. - Current Feature : - Home page of Aesthetic Centre - Digital Showcase - Request Item - Initiate Item Return - Onlne Toggle - User Directory - Tags for verified User - Future scope - Machine log (Machine Online and Offline) - Student RFID log - Fab Academy Graduate - Focus Area under the Aesthetic and Technology Domain

docs.title: Machine Bookings:
docs.text: Manual maintaining the log is necessary at this point of 3D printing, laser cutter and vinyl cutter.

docs.title: Project Proposals:
docs.text:
Submit your idea, sketch, and resource list for approval.

  • Examples:

Tsheltrim Lhamo

Anith Ghalley

Thinley Wozer Dorji

Damzang Chimi Selden

docs.title: Personal Storage Boxes:
docs.text: Each project group gets a designated box. Everything goes in the box, and the box goes on the shelf. Without a designated box, components will not be provided

docs.title: Component List.
docs.text: Backed by research on what kind of components do you need and why? Whats the purpose of thise components in your project.

docs.title: Questions to Answer
docs.text:
• What will your Project do?
  • Who’s done what beforehand? [Research]
  • What will you design?
  • What materials and components will be used?
  • What will be the estimate cost of your Project?
  • What are your key-question to complete your project?

docs.title: The User Forum:
docs.text:
Ideation and Guidance

A space where interested student should be able to meet monthly to share about their progress and ideas and get inputs from the senior students and teachers.

docs.title: Digital Archive:
docs.text: A place where all the documentation and projects can be archived for others to access as well. - Git hub Pages - Aesthetic Centre Website

docs.title: 📦 4. Physical Organization: The Aesthetic Centre
docs.text: A messy lab is a can be a sign of work happening in the Lab but accountability must be maintained. We are implementing strict record keeping to keep our space professional and sustainable

docs.title: Project Display
docs.text:
The Mini Museum: Completed projects aren't left on tables; they are displayed in a sorted, styled museum area.

Figure 1: Student Led Session

The Foot Mat & Cleaning Rule: No mud on laptops! A deep clean happens once a week by the incharges.

docs.title: Laptops:
docs.text:
Figure 1: Student Led Session

Labeling System: * Laptops: Numbered and tracked with stickers. If a student is using the laptop then they will have to toggle on the switch on the Web app. Damaged Laptops must be reported as soon as possible

Waste: Segregated into Reusable Scraps (for keychains!) and Non-reusable. which can be disposed.

image of useful material for laser cutting

image of the disposable material for laser cutting

docs.title: 🛡️ 5. The Do’s and Don’ts (The Core Team Code)
docs.text: Strict rules ensure creative freedom and accessibility

docs.title: ✅ DO:
docs.text:
Independent Use: Incharges can use facilities independently to build their skills.

Safety First: Always wear goggles/gloves when the machines are humming.

Report Hazards: If a tool breaks, report it immediately. No blame, just fix it.

Continuous Learning: Use lab laptops to take online courses and level up.

Make sure if students are using machine, it is being recorded and they follow proper procedure.

Make sure all the users who are using the laptop has turned on the toggle switch on the webapp

Conduct meetings and take minutes of the meeting.

docs.title: ❌ DON’T:
docs.text:
No Overnights: The lab closes strictly at 9:30 PM unless if teachers are available

No Guest Stars: Do not bring unauthorized friends during night study.

The No-Exit Policy: Laptops and tools never leave the FabLab room.

Personal Projects: Resources are for educational and innovative growth, not personal chores.

docs.title: 🚀 6. The Project Journey (Standard Workflow)
docs.text:
Follow these 4 steps to go from Idea to Expert.

The Pitch: Submit a proposal (Idea + Sketch + Steps + Resources).

The Approval: A Teachers verifies your plan.

The Build: Use the tools safely. Log your usage in the Log Book.

The Legacy: Document your work, return all tools, and display your project in the Mini Museum.

💡 Pro Tip: Students who consistently complete projects and follow rules earn Verified User status, unlocking advanced machine access!

docs.title: 🚀 Getting Started Guide
docs.text: This page demonstrates all the Markdown features available in this template. Use it as a reference when writing your own documentation!

docs.title: 📝 Text Formatting
docs.text:
You can use bold, italic, strikethrough, highlighted, and inline code.

You can also write underlined text and use footnotes1.

docs.title: Unordered List
docs.text:
• Item one
  • Item two
    • Nested item A
    • Nested item B
  • Item three

docs.title: Ordered List
docs.text:
1. First step
  2. Second step
  3. Third step

docs.title: Task List
docs.text:
• Set up MkDocs
  • Customize theme
  • Write documentation
  • Deploy to GitHub Pages

docs.title: 💡 Admonitions (Callout Boxes)
docs.text:
This is a Note

Use notes to provide additional context or background information.

Helpful Tip

Use tips to share useful advice, shortcuts, or best practices.

Watch Out

Use warnings to highlight potential issues or pitfalls.

Critical Warning

Use danger boxes for critical information that could cause data loss or errors.

Example

Use example boxes to show worked examples or demonstrations.

Collapsible Section (click to expand)

This content is hidden by default. Use ??? instead of !!! to make any admonition collapsible — great for long details that not everyone needs.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def greet(name: str) -> str:
    """Return a greeting message."""
    return f"Hello, {name}! Welcome to MkDocs."

print(greet("Student"))

docs.title: JavaScript
docs.text:
function fibonacci(n) {
  if (n <= 1) return n;
  return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2);
}

console.log(fibonacci(10)); // 55

docs.title: Arduino / C++
docs.text:
void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  pinMode(LED_BUILTIN, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, HIGH);
  delay(1000);
  digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, LOW);
  delay(1000);
}

docs.title: 📊 Tables
docs.text: Feature Status Notes Navigation Tabs ✅ Done Four tabs configured Dark Mode ✅ Done Toggle in header Search ✅ Done Built-in search plugin Code Highlight ✅ Done Multiple languages Deployment ⏳ Pending GitHub Pages ready

docs.title: 🔀 Tabbed Content
docs.text:
Use tabs to organize alternative content — great for showing code in different languages or steps for different operating systems.

WindowsmacOS / LinuxDocker 
pip install mkdocs-material
mkdocs serve

pip3 install mkdocs-material
mkdocs serve

docker pull squidfunk/mkdocs-material
docker run --rm -it -p 8000:8000 -v ${PWD}:/docs squidfunk/mkdocs-material

docs.title: 🖼️ Embedding Images from Google Drive
docs.text:
To embed an image stored in Google Drive:

  1. Upload your image to Google Drive
  2. Share it: Right-click → Share → Change to "Anyone with the link"
  3. Copy the link — it will look like: 
    https://drive.google.com/file/d/YOUR_FILE_ID/view?usp=sharing
  4. Extract the FILE_ID (the long string after /d/)
  5. Use this Markdown in your .md file:
![Image Description](https://drive.google.com/thumbnail?id=YOUR_FILE_ID&sz=w800)

Image Size Control

Change sz=w800 to adjust width: - sz=w400 — small - sz=w800 — medium - sz=w1200 — large

docs.title: Example (replace with your image)
docs.text: ⬆️ Uncomment the block above and replace YOUR_FILE_ID to see your image!

docs.text:
• MkDocs Documentation
  • Material for MkDocs
  • Markdown Cheat Sheet
  • PyMdown Extensions

docs.title: 📐 Diagrams with Mermaid
docs.text:
graph LR
    A["Start"] --> B["Write Docs"]
    B --> C{"Happy?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Deploy!"]
    C -- No --> B

That's it! Copy any of these elements into your own pages. Happy documenting! 🎉

  1. This is a footnote — useful for references and citations! ↩

docs.title: 🚀 How to Deploy Your Site on GitHub Pages
docs.text: This guide walks you through publishing your MkDocs documentation site on the internet using GitHub — for free. Follow every step in order.

docs.title: What You Will Need
docs.text:
Requirement Where to get it A GitHub account github.com/signup This repository Your teacher will share the GitHub repo link

No Python or software installation is needed if you use Codespaces.

docs.title: Step 1 — Fork or Copy the Repository
docs.text:
Your teacher will give you one of these options:

Option A — You already have the repoOption B — Fork it yourself

Your teacher has already added you as a collaborator. Skip to Step 2.

  1. Open the GitHub repo link your teacher shared
  2. Click the Fork button (top-right corner)
  3. Keep the default settings and click Create fork
  4. You now have your own copy — continue to Step 2

docs.title: Step 2 — Enable GitHub Pages
docs.text:
Do this ONCE before your first deployment

  1. Open your repository on GitHub
  2. Click Settings (the cog icon in the top menu)
  3. In the left sidebar, click Pages
  4. Under Source, choose:
  5. Deploy from a branch
  6. Branch: gh-pages
  7. Folder: / (root)
  8. Click Save

Note

The gh-pages branch is created automatically when the first deployment runs. If you don't see it yet, complete Step 4 first, then come back and set this.

docs.title: Step 3 — Edit Your Site
docs.text:
You have two ways to edit your site. Codespaces is strongly recommended.

🌐 Codespaces (No installation — works in your browser)💻 Local Machine (requires Python installed)
  1. Open your repository on GitHub
  2. Click the green Code button
  3. Click the Codespaces tab

  4. Click New codespace

  5. Wait 1–2 minutes for the environment to build

  6. A live preview of your site opens automatically in the browser panel

To edit a page:

  1. In the file explorer on the left, open the docs/ folder
  2. Click any .md file (e.g. docs/about/index.md)
  3. Make your changes — the live preview updates instantly!
# 1. Clone your repository
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO.git
cd YOUR-REPO

# 2. Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 3. Start the live preview
mkdocs serve

# 4. Open in your browser:
#    http://127.0.0.1:8000

docs.title: Step 4 — Save and Publish Your Changes
docs.text:
Every time you save and push your changes to GitHub, the site rebuilds automatically.

In CodespacesOn your local machine
  1. Press Ctrl+S (or Cmd+S on Mac) to save your file
  2. Click the Source Control icon in the left sidebar (it looks like a branch)
  3. In the Message box, type a short description of your change Example: Update my About Me page
  4. Click the ✔ Commit button (tick/checkmark)
  5. Click Sync Changes (or Push)

Tip

You can also use the terminal inside Codespaces: 

git add .
git commit -m "Your message here"
git push


git add .
git commit -m "Your message here"
git push

docs.title: Step 5 — Watch the Deployment Run
docs.text:
1. Go to your repository on GitHub
  2. Click the Actions tab
  3. You will see a workflow called Deploy MkDocs to GitHub Pages running
  4. Click on it to watch the progress
  5. When it shows a green tick ✅, your site is live!

How long does it take?

Usually 1–3 minutes from when you push your changes.

docs.title: Step 6 — Visit Your Live Website
docs.text:
Your site URL follows this pattern:

https://YOUR-USERNAME.github.io/YOUR-REPO-NAME/

Example: if your GitHub username is ahmed and the repo is my-docs: 

https://ahmed.github.io/my-docs/

You can find the exact URL in Settings → Pages (it appears at the top once the site is live).

docs.title: 📝 What to Edit
docs.text: File What it controls mkdocs.yml Site name, your GitHub URL, social links docs/index.md Home page welcome message docs/about/index.md Your name, bio, skills, photo docs/documentation/ Your notes and guides docs/projects/ Your project writeups

docs.title: Changing Your Site Name
docs.text:
Open mkdocs.yml and update the top lines:

site_name: Ahmed's Documentation    # ← Your name/project
site_url: https://ahmed.github.io/my-docs/
site_author: Ahmed Al-Rashid

repo_url: https://github.com/ahmed/my-docs
repo_name: ahmed/my-docs

docs.title: Adding a New Page
docs.text:
1. Create a new .md file inside docs/documentation/
  2. Add it to mkdocs.yml under the nav: section:
nav:
  - Documentation:
      - Overview: documentation/index.md
      - My New Topic: documentation/my-new-topic.md   # ← add this

docs.title: ❓ Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Problem Solution Actions tab shows a red ❌ Click the failed run → read the error log → check your mkdocs.yml for typos Site URL shows 404 Go to Settings → Pages and confirm source is set to gh-pages branch Changes not showing Wait 2–3 minutes and refresh. If still not updated, check the Actions tab Codespace preview not opening In the terminal, run mkdocs serve --dev-addr=0.0.0.0:8000 manually

You're done!

Your site is now live on the internet and updates automatically every time you push a change. 🎉

docs.title: Installing Design Software
docs.text:
• Verify that the computer meets the minimum hardware requirements for software installation.
    • Note: Hardware requirements depend on the kind of software.
  • Open official websites of the softwares (inkscape, gimp, etc)
  • Select the free or trial version of the software
  • Download according to the OS of the computer
  • Follow up the steps given in the software website.
  • Open the software and verify the installation.
  • Restore tools, material and equipment
  • Clean the work area

Different softwares in Fab Lab. Each Software name is hyperlinked

docs.title: GIMP (GNU Image Manipulation Program)
docs.text:
It is a completely free and open-source application primarily used for editing and manipulating photographs and raster-based images.

The software supports various image formats and offers tools for photo retouching, image composition, and image authoring.

Personally, I found myself having a lot of fun exploring its various filters and tools to see how they could alter my pictures.

docs.title: Inkscape
docs.text:
This is a professional, free vector graphics editor that allows you to create and modify scalable illustrations and designs using paths rather than pixels.

The interface presented a bit of a learning curve at first, but I quickly discovered that it becomes quite user-friendly once you understand the basics of vector paths and node editing.

I found an online tutorial that was particularly helpful for grasping the bezier curve tools and path operations like union, difference, and intersection.

docs.title: Fusion 360
docs.text:
• For this tool you can reach out to Mr. Anith to get educational access to you can apply yourself and it will be valid for a year

  • A cloud-based integrated software platform used for industrial and mechanical design, 3D modeling, simulation, and CAD/CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing). The software enables the creation of assemblies, and technical drawings for fabrication.

docs.title: Blender
docs.text:
A powerful, free, and open-source 3D creation suite that supports the entirety of the 3D pipeline, including modeling, rigging, animation, simulation, rendering, compositing, and motion tracking.

It operates primarily with mesh based geometry rather than parametric solids, offering different modeling strategies like subdivision surfaces and sculpting.

It is widely used for artistic projects, game assets, and creating realistic visual effects.

docs.title: Onshape
docs.text: Unlike installed software, Onshape is a web based design platform that enables users to create precise 2D sketches and complex 3D models directly within a browser using cloud native architecture. It is a common choice for collaborative engineering projects due to its real time multi-user editing capabilities and automatic version control.

docs.title: Tinkercad
docs.text:
A browser based, entry-level 3D design tool that uses a block building approach similar to digital LEGO construction.

It is particularly accessible for beginners and educational settings, offering a gentle introduction to 3D modeling concepts before advancing to more complex software.

The platform also includes basic electronic circuit simulation and code-block programming capabilities.

docs.title: FFmpeg
docs.text:
For compressing my video documentation to meet upload requirements and manage file sizes efficiently, I utilized FFmpeg.

It is a leading, open-source multimedia framework designed to handle video, audio, and other media files, typically operated through a command-line interface.

The software supports a vast array of codecs and formats, allowing for precise control over compression parameters, bitrates, and resolution.

docs.title: Wokwi
docs.text:
Wokwi is a free, browser-based electronics simulator that lets you design and test circuits virtually without any physical hardware.

It supports a wide range of microcontrollers including Arduino, ESP32, and Raspberry Pi Pico, along with common components like LEDs, sensors, displays, and motors.

You can write and upload code directly in the simulator, see your circuit run in real-time, and debug everything before building it in real life — making it especially useful for learning, prototyping, and testing ideas when you don't have components on hand.

docs.title: Arduino IDE
docs.text:
Arduino IDE is a free, open source integrated development environment that makes it easy to write, compile, and upload code to Arduino compatible microcontrollers.

It uses a simplified version of C++ with built-in functions that handle low level hardware details, making embedded programming accessible for beginners while still powerful enough for advanced users.

docs.title: Arduino and Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)
docs.text: Here's a beginner-friendly Arduino LCD Reference Manual that introduces both 16-pin standard LCDs and 4-pin I2C LCDs using Arduino UNO. This is written with the assumption that students are just getting started, so it includes relevant terminologies and suggested keywords for further research.

docs.title: Blink code for arduino
docs.text: you can test the defualt blink code in arduino under file tab.

docs.title: 📘 Arduino LCD Reference Manual
docs.text: For Students Exploring Display Interfaces Using Arduino UNO Prepared by: [Your Name, STEM Teacher]

docs.title: 📌 What You Will Learn:
docs.text:
• Introduction to LCDs and Arduino
  • Components & Tools Needed
  • Understanding LCD Types: 16-pin vs I2C
  • Step-by-Step Wiring Guide
  • Arduino Code Examples
  • Troubleshooting Tips

docs.title: 🧠 1. Basic Terminologies
docs.text:
Before starting, here are a few key terms you’ll encounter:

Term Meaning Search Keyword LCD Liquid Crystal Display “LCD 16x2 Arduino” Pin A connection point on a component “Arduino Pinout” I2C A communication protocol used for sensors/modules “I2C Communication Arduino” Potentiometer A variable resistor used to adjust brightness “Arduino LCD Contrast” Breadboard A board for prototyping electronics “Breadboard Arduino Projects” Sketch A program written in Arduino IDE “Arduino Sketch Examples”

docs.title: 🔧 2. Components Required
docs.text:
For both types of LCDs, you will need:

  • Arduino UNO

  • USB cable (Type B) for Arduino

  • LCD 16x2 module (16-pin)

  • I2C LCD module (4-pin)

  • Breadboard and jumper wires

  • 10kΩ potentiometer (for 16-pin LCD)

  • Resistors (if needed for backlight)

  • Arduino IDE installed on your computer

docs.title: 🖥️ 3. Setting Up the Arduino IDE
docs.text: Before coding:

docs.title: With Hardware
docs.text:
Download and install the Arduino IDE from https://www.arduino.cc/en/software.

Connect the Arduino UNO to your laptop using a USB cable.

Open Arduino IDE and select:

Board: Arduino UNO

Port: COM port assigned to the Arduino

you can use the Wok-wi or tinkercad simulation

🔌 4. Interfacing 16x2 LCD (16-pin) 🔩 Pin Mapping | LCD Pin | Label | Connects To | |-------------|-----------|-------------------------------------------| | 1 | GND | GND on Arduino | | 2 | VCC | 5V on Arduino | | 3 | VO | Middle pin of potentiometer | | 4 | RS | Digital Pin 12 | | 5 | RW | GND | | 6 | E | Digital Pin 11 | | 7–10 | D0–D3 | Not connected (used in 8-bit mode only) | | 11–14 | D4–D7 | Digital Pins 5, 4, 3, 2 | | 15 | LED+ | 220Ω resistor to 5V | | 16 | LED- | GND |

🧑‍💻 Sample Code for 16-Pin LCD 

#include <LiquidCrystal.h>

// RS, E, D4, D5, D6, D7
LiquidCrystal lcd(12, 11, 5, 4, 3, 2);

void setup() {
  lcd.begin(16, 2); // 16 columns and 2 rows
  lcd.print("Hello, World!");
}

void loop() {
  // nothing here
}

docs.title: Display On and Off
docs.text:
This example sketch shows how to use the display() and noDisplay() methods to turn on and off the display.

#include <LiquidCrystal.h>

// RS, E, D4, D5, D6, D7
LiquidCrystal lcd(12, 11, 5, 4, 3, 2);

void setup() {
  lcd.begin(16, 2); // 16 columns and 2 rows
  lcd.print("Hello, World!");
}

void loop() {
  lcd.noDisplay();
  delay(500);
  // Turn on the display:
  lcd.display();
  delay(500);
}

docs.title: Display with Timmer.
docs.text:
#include <LiquidCrystal.h>

// RS, E, D4, D5, D6, D7
LiquidCrystal lcd(12, 11, 5, 4, 3, 2);

void setup() {
  lcd.begin(16, 2); // 16 columns and 2 rows
  lcd.print("Hello, World!");
}

void loop() {
  // set the cursor to column 0, line 1
  // (note: line 1 is the second row, since counting begins with 0):
  lcd.setCursor(0, 1);
  // print the number of seconds since reset:
  lcd.print(millis() / 1000);
}

🔌 5. Interfacing 16x2 LCD Using I2C (4-pin) The I2C module is soldered to the back of the LCD, reducing pin usage.

🔩 Pin Mapping I2C Pin Label Connects To GND Ground GND on Arduino VCC Power 5V on Arduino SDA Serial Data A4 on Arduino SCL Serial Clock A5 on Arduino

⚙️ Install I2C Library Open Arduino IDE → Sketch → Include Library → Manage Libraries…

Search and install: LiquidCrystal I2C (by Frank de Brabander or similar)

🧑‍💻 Sample Code for I2C LCD

#include <Wire.h>
#include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h>

// LCD address is usually 0x27 or 0x3F
LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2);  

void setup() {
  lcd.init();            
  lcd.backlight();       
  lcd.print("Hello I2C LCD");
}

void loop() {
  // nothing here
}

⚠️ 6. Common Troubleshooting Tips | Issue | Solution | |------------------------|-----------------------------------------------| | LCD shows no text | Adjust potentiometer (for 16-pin) | | I2C LCD not working | Check address using I2C Scanner sketch | | Garbage text | Ensure correct wiring & baud rate | | No power | Recheck GND and VCC connections |

🧭 7. Keywords for Further Learning You can learn more by searching these topics:

"Arduino LCD vs I2C"

"Arduino LCD Potentiometer Contrast"

"Arduino I2C Scanner"

"Arduino LCD scrolling text"

"Custom characters on LCD Arduino"

✅ Final Notes Using the 4-pin I2C LCD simplifies wiring but requires the I2C library and address check.

The 16-pin LCD gives you hands-on experience with control and data pins.

Always power off your Arduino before re-wiring.

Ask your teacher if you want to try custom animations, sensor data display, or interactive menus using LCDs.

Reference : Tsheltrim Lhamo , Tsheyang Palki Tshewang.

docs.title: 🏗️ Projects Overview
docs.text: Welcome to my project portfolio! Each project includes detailed documentation covering objectives, implementation, and results.

docs.title: 🔧 Project Template
docs.text:
Template

A starter template for documenting your project. Copy this to create new project entries.

Tech Stack: Tool 1 · Tool 2 · Tool 3

View Project Details

docs.title: ➕ How to Add New Projects
docs.text:
Adding a New Project

  1. Copy project-template.md and rename it (e.g., smart-home-system.md)

  2. Fill in your project details using the template structure

  3. Add a card above by copying a project card block

  4. Update mkdocs.yml — add your new page:

    nav:
  - Projects:
      - Overview: projects/index.md
      - "📋 Project Template": projects/project-template.md
      - Smart Home System: projects/smart-home-system.md  # ← add here

  5. Run mkdocs serve and check it out!

docs.title: 🔧 Project Title
docs.text:
In Progress

Duration: Month Year – Month Year Role: Your Role Team Size: Solo / Number of members Repository: GitHub Link

docs.title: 📋 Overview
docs.text:
Write a concise overview of your project. What problem does it solve? Who is it for? What makes it interesting?

Project Summary

A 2-3 sentence summary of the project that gives readers a quick understanding of what this is about.

docs.title: 🎯 Objectives
docs.text:
1. Primary Goal — What is the main thing this project achieves?
  2. Secondary Goal — Any additional objectives
  3. Learning Goal — What did you learn through this project?

docs.title: 🏛️ Architecture / Design
docs.text:
Describe the high-level architecture or design of your project. You can use a Mermaid diagram:

graph TD
    A["Input"] --> B["Processing"]
    B --> C["Module 1"]
    B --> D["Module 2"]
    C --> E["Output"]
    D --> E

docs.text:
1. Reference Title — Brief note on what it covers
  2. Another Reference — Brief note
  3. Documentation — Brief note
